risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "To match the two groups, randomization was stratified according to the body regions of the primary surgery (Table I). Subsequently, randomization was performed when the patient was in the anesthetic room immediately before surgery by using the sealed envelope method." Comment: The study reports stratified randomization, which is a context clue indicating a systematic random allocation process rather than a non-random rule. Although the exact sequence-generation tool (e.g. computer or random number table) is not specified, the description of stratified randomization supports that a proper random sequence was used.

Quote: "Subsequently, randomization was performed when the patient was in the anesthetic room immediately before surgery by using the sealed envelope method." Comment: The trial reports use of sealed envelopes, but does not specify that the envelopes were sequentially numbered, opaque, and sealed. Because not all required SNOSE features are explicitly described, allocation concealment cannot be confirmed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The surgical team in theatre were aware of group allocation, but only following the induction of anesthesia." Quote: "The secondary outcomes of interest were ... pain level 7 days after surgery [measured by the visual analog scale (VAS)] ..." Comment: Key personnel delivering the intervention were explicitly unblinded. Although follow-up clinicians were blinded, this does not address performance bias. Because the study included outcomes that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, especially patient-reported pain and aspects of postoperative care that may affect healing time or length of stay, the lack of blinding of personnel represents high risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "clinicians undertaking the follow-up of postoperative wounds were fully blinded to the group assignments." and "The secondary outcomes of interest were ... pain level 7 days after surgery [measured by the visual analog scale (VAS)]". Comment: The wound follow-up assessor was a blinded clinician, which reduces detection bias for clinician-assessed outcomes such as wound healing. However, the study also included a subjective patient-reported outcome (VAS pain), for which the patient is the outcome assessor. In this surgical comparison (endoscopy vs traditional open surgery with different postoperative management), participants were not reported as blinded and were likely aware of the intervention received after surgery. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of an unblinded subjective self-reported outcome makes this domain high risk overall.

Quote: "we enrolled 106 patients with serious SSI in our study and randomized 57 to the endoscopy procedure group (group A) and 49 to the traditional surgery group (group B). No patients were lost during the follow-up period." and "All the patients enrolled were followed up for at least 4 weeks following surgery" and Table II reports outcomes for both groups, including "Rate of skin transplantation 3/57" vs "6/49". Comment: Post-randomization follow-up appears complete for the analysed outcomes, with 0% attrition and no imbalance between arms; outcome denominators are consistent with the randomized group sizes.
